question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 118km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 14 units of medical kit (10kg each) and 2 units of construction material (70kg each). The boat can travel at 50km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.

From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 27km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 56min, 3rd: 62min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 104km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (23kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 12%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 36km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 46min, 3rd: 52min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (14kg each) and 3 units of construction material (70kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 26km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.

From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 9 units of relief supply box (63kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 51km/h in still water.

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 112km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of medical kit (11kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (53kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.

From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 56km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 7km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 61min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 118km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (7kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.

From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 99km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 44min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 88km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (55kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 24km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 48km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 40 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 42min, 3rd: 50min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 105km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 8 units of water barrel (23kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 40km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.

From 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 45km waypoint, 5 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 38min, 3rd: 43min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 121km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (20kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (9kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 26km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 84km waypoint, 7 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 7km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 55min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 100km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 6 units of water barrel (22kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (61kg each). The boat can travel at 50km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 36km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.

From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 10%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 64km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 40 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 52min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 120km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of water barrel (15kg each) and 3 units of construction material (75kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.

From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 42km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 7km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 35min, 3rd: 41min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (5kg each). The boat can travel at 52km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 38km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 56km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 91km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (73kg each) and 2 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

From 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 36km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 25 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 25min, 2nd: 31min, 3rd: 37min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 109km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (23kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.

From 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 64km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 40 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 58min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 113km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 36km waypoint, 5 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 26 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 26min, 2nd: 31min, 3rd: 36min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (22kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 119km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of construction material (53kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (22kg each). The boat can travel at 51km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 37km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.

From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 90km waypoint, 7 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 113km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (63kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (65kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.

From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 10%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 90km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 46 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 42min, 3rd: 49min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (54kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 121km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of water barrel (16kg each) and 6 units of construction material (66kg each). The boat can travel at 46km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 31 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 31min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 47min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 111km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 6 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 4 units of construction material (80kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 54km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 115km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 6 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 101km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 5 units of construction material (63kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (15kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 81km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 27 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 27min, 2nd: 35min, 3rd: 43min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 8 units of relief supply box (41kg each) and 2 units of construction material (59kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 92km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 7 units of construction material (54kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (21kg each). The boat can travel at 47km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.

From 10:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 63km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 59min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 110km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.

From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 40km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 41 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 56min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 116km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 7 units of construction material (63kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (66kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.

At the 96km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 116km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 7 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 4 units of construction material (68kg each). The boat can travel at 40km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 45km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 58min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 92km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (7kg each) and 4 units of construction material (62kg each). The boat can travel at 50km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.

From 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 63km waypoint, 6 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 37min, 3rd: 42min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 94km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 7 units of relief supply box (36kg each) and 6 units of construction material (77kg each). The boat can travel at 53km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.

From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 81km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 61min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 7 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 2 units of construction material (54kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 101km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 4 units of construction material (69kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 26km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 90km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 40 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 30 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 30min, 2nd: 37min, 3rd: 44min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 7 units of construction material (75kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 26km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.

From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 90km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 40 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 60min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 113km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 6 units of construction material (62kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (12kg each). The boat can travel at 41km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.

From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 45km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 65min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 102km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 11 units of medical kit (11kg each) and 3 units of relief supply box (42kg each). The boat can travel at 47km/h in still water.

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 91km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (30kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 46km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 45km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 28 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 28min, 2nd: 33min, 3rd: 38min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 90km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 7 units of water barrel (19kg each) and 2 units of construction material (61kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 13% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.

From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 72km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 52min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of water barrel (19kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (50kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 104km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of water barrel (22kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2200kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 12%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 32km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 41 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 37min, 3rd: 45min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 107km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 7 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 2 units of construction material (59kg each). The boat can travel at 51km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.

From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 56km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 40 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 8km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 58min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 100km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (63kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (54kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.

From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 45km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 34 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 5 minutes (1st: 34min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 44min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (57kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (16kg each). The boat can travel at 46km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 15%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 81km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 32 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 32min, 2nd: 38min, 3rd: 44min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 101km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (76kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (17kg each). The boat can travel at 50km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 10%.

From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 10%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 72km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 45min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
